title: ACLS – Post-Cardiac Arrest Care and Targeted Temperature Management
description: A patient achieves ROSC after a witnessed out-of-hospital VFib arrest but remains comatose. Practise comprehensive post-cardiac arrest care: oxygenation targets, haemodynamic optimisation, targeted temperature management (TTM), and neuroprognostication.
learningObjectives: Apply post-cardiac arrest care bundle (oxygenation, haemodynamics, TTM, 12-lead ECG)
Maintain MAP ≥ 65 mmHg post-ROSC to optimise cerebral perfusion
Describe the indications and temperature targets for TTM
Understand the timing of neuroprognostication after cardiac arrest

states.prompt: A 55-year-old male achieved ROSC after 22 minutes of resuscitation for witnessed out-of-hospital VFib. He is intubated, comatose (GCS 3). BP 88/52. HR 118. SpO2 99% on FiO2 100%. EtCO2 42 mmHg. 12-lead ECG shows no ST changes. What is the MOST urgent immediate post-ROSC intervention?

options.label: Reduce FiO2 to target SpO2 94–98% (avoid hyperoxia)
Keep FiO2 100% – maximum O2 is safe post-arrest
Perform urgent CT head to assess neurological damage
Start ice packs immediately without further assessment

explanations: Correct! Hyperoxia (SpO2 100% on FiO2 100%) post-ROSC generates reactive oxygen species and worsens reperfusion injury. Target SpO2 94–98% by titrating FiO2 down.
Hyperoxia is independently associated with worse neurological outcomes post-ROSC. FiO2 should be titrated down to target 94–98%.
CT head is important but secondary to immediate physiological stabilisation. Haemodynamics and oxygenation take priority in the first minutes.
TTM requires proper indication assessment and controlled cooling – not immediate ice packs without assessment.

checklistItems.description: Correctly reduces FiO2 to avoid hyperoxia post-ROSC

states.prompt: FiO2 titrated to 0.45, SpO2 96%. BP remains 82/50 (MAP 61 mmHg). No STEMI on ECG. What MAP target is recommended post-ROSC and how should it be achieved in this patient?

options.label: MAP ≥ 65 mmHg; administer vasopressors (norepinephrine or dopamine) and IV fluid if tolerated
MAP ≥ 50 mmHg is sufficient in post-arrest patients
Aim MAP ≥ 80 mmHg with aggressive fluid resuscitation
Allow permissive hypotension and wait for spontaneous haemodynamic improvement

explanations: Correct! AHA guidelines recommend MAP ≥ 65 mmHg post-ROSC to maintain cerebral and coronary perfusion. Vasopressors (norepinephrine preferred) with cautious fluids are the correct management.
MAP ≥ 50 mmHg is insufficient to maintain cerebral autoregulation in the post-arrest brain.
Aggressive fluid resuscitation risks pulmonary oedema especially with post-arrest myocardial dysfunction. Vasopressors are preferred over large fluid volumes.
Permissive hypotension is not appropriate post-ROSC. The post-arrest brain has impaired autoregulation and requires sustained MAP ≥ 65 mmHg.

checklistItems.description: Correctly targets MAP ≥ 65 mmHg with vasopressors post-ROSC

states.prompt: BP now 102/64 (MAP 77 mmHg) on norepinephrine. The patient remains comatose (GCS 4). He was resuscitated from VFib at a shockable initial rhythm. What is the current recommendation for targeted temperature management (TTM)?

options.label: Prevent fever (temperature ≥ 37.7°C) for 72 hours; active cooling to 32–36°C may be considered
Cool aggressively to 32°C immediately – this is mandatory for all post-arrest patients
TTM is no longer recommended; normothermia is the only goal
Warm the patient to 38°C to promote neurological recovery

explanations: Correct! 2021 AHA guidelines (ILCOR advisory): active fever prevention (≤37.7°C) is mandatory for ≥72 hrs. Active cooling to 32–36°C is reasonable (individual patient consideration). Hyperthermia worsens neurological outcomes.
Post-TTM trial (2021), mandatory cooling to 32°C is no longer universally required. Fever prevention and individualised cooling (32–36°C) is the current recommendation.
Fever prevention (≤37.7°C) remains a strong recommendation. 'No TTM' is incorrect — temperature control is essential.
Warming to 38°C would be fever and is definitively harmful for post-arrest brain. Never intentionally induce fever post-arrest.

checklistItems.description: Correctly describes current TTM recommendation (fever prevention + individualised cooling)

states.prompt: Day 3 post-arrest: temperature controlled at 36°C. The patient is off sedation for 24 hours. GCS motor score remains 1 (no motor response). The family asks about the prognosis. When is neuroprognostication most reliable?

options.label: ≥72 hours after arrest AND ≥24 hours after rewarming; use multimodal assessment (EEG, SSEP, CT/MRI, biomarkers)
24 hours post-arrest is sufficient for accurate prognosis
GCS score alone at 48 hours is sufficient for prognostication
Neurological outcome cannot be predicted post-cardiac arrest

explanations: Correct! Reliable neuroprognostication requires ≥72 hours after arrest AND ≥24 hours after rewarming. Multimodal assessment includes: clinical exam, EEG (burst suppression), SSEP (N20 absence), CT/MRI, serum NSE/S100B.
24-hour prognosis is unreliable — sedation and hypothermia confound neurological examination. Early prognostication can lead to self-fulfilling prophecies.
GCS alone is an unreliable prognostic tool. Absent pupillary reflexes, absent N20 SSEP, or burst suppression EEG provide stronger negative predictors.
Prognostication is possible with multimodal tools, though uncertainty remains. It is unethical to provide no prognostic guidance to families.

checklistItems.description: Correctly identifies ≥72 hr multimodal neuroprognostication timeline

states.prompt:
Scenario complete. Review your performance summary below.

📚 Key Teaching Points:
• Post-ROSC: avoid hyperoxia (SpO2 94–98%), target MAP ≥ 65 mmHg with vasopressors
• TTM (2021): mandatory fever prevention ≤37.7°C × 72 hrs; cooling to 32–36°C individualised
• Hyperthermia post-arrest is definitively harmful
• Neuroprognostication: wait ≥72 hrs post-arrest, ≥24 hrs post-rewarming, multimodal assessment
